title: Design Heuristics
source: Compiled from widely used UX heuristics and standards (NN/g heuristics; ISO 9241-110 interaction principles; WCAG 2.2; Material Design usability/accessibility guidance).
purpose: Provide reference knowledge for evaluating and improving UI/UX quality using established heuristics and standards. Intended for design critique, QA reviews, discovery, and inspiration across product, design, engineering, and content workflows.
scope: Applies to user-facing interfaces across web and mobile: interaction design, layout, navigation, forms, feedback, states, accessibility, and content clarity. Excludes assistant behavior, tone control, output formatting, JSON/schema enforcement, and runtime execution rules.
definitions: A general design principle used to evaluate interface usability and identify likely issues (especially effective for quick expert reviews).
A widely used set of 10 principles for interface design evaluation (e.g., visibility of system status, consistency and standards, error prevention).
International standard principles describing qualities of usable interactive systems (e.g., suitability for the task, self-descriptiveness, controllability, conformity with user expectations).
Accessibility principles: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust; used to evaluate whether experiences work for people with disabilities and assistive tech.
rulesConstraints: Prefer evidence-based critique: tie findings to observed UI elements, user goals, and a specific heuristic or principle.
Prioritize by user impact and frequency: address blockers (task failure), then high-friction issues, then polish.
Recommend fixes as concrete design changes (what to change, where, and how), not vague advice.
Always evaluate key states where applicable: empty, loading, error, success, disabled, focus, hover, offline, and partial success.
Assume constraints: optimize for clarity and recoverability rather than perfect completeness when context is limited.
Accessibility baseline: evaluate against WCAG principles and common AA expectations (e.g., contrast, keyboard, focus visibility, labels).
Avoid conflicting guidance: when two heuristics trade off (e.g., minimalist design vs discoverability), choose the option that best supports the user’s task and reduces critical errors.

do: Map each finding to a heuristic (Nielsen/ISO/WCAG) to make critiques consistent and teachable.
Check navigation and wayfinding: users should always know where they are, what happened, and what to do next.
Check recognition over recall: reduce memory load with visible options, previews, defaults, and sensible constraints.
Check control and freedom: provide undo/back/cancel, safe exits, and reversible actions for risky steps.
Check consistency: patterns, components, spacing, terminology, and behaviors should match within the product and platform conventions.
Check feedback loops: show system status, progress, and confirmations promptly and proportionately.
Check error prevention + recovery: prevent mistakes; when failures happen, explain what happened and how to recover.
Check accessibility early: labels, focus order, keyboard access, contrast, and error announcement patterns.
dont: Don’t give generic feedback like “improve hierarchy” without pointing to specific elements and changes.
Don’t optimize aesthetics at the expense of comprehension, task success, and accessibility.
Don’t hide critical actions, costs, or consequences behind ambiguous UI or copy.
Don’t require users to remember information between steps when it can be shown or inferred.
Don’t rely on color alone to communicate meaning (errors, selected states, statuses).
Don’t introduce new interaction patterns without a clear user benefit and consistent application.

examples: Finding: A Save action provides no feedback; users are unsure if it worked. Fix: Add inline ‘Saved’ confirmation and/or a toast; for long operations show progress and disable repeated submission.
Finding: Users must remember filter criteria after navigating back. Fix: Persist filter chips and show active filters above results with clear reset controls.
### Example: Controllability + undo (ISO / Nielsen User control & freedom)
Finding: Deleting an item is immediate with no undo. Fix: Provide an undo snackbar/toast for a short window; for destructive actions require confirmation with clear consequences.

Finding: Form errors are only shown in a red banner at top; screen reader users don’t hear field-level guidance. Fix: Add field-level messages bound to inputs, focus the first invalid field on submit, and ensure errors are announced.
edgeCases: When selection context is missing, provide general heuristics and ask for the specific screen, flow, or element to evaluate.
When reviewing a single component inside a larger screen, evaluate local usability first, then note likely upstream/downstream dependencies (validation, empty states, permissions).
When designs are wireframes, prioritize structure, flow, and clarity over visual polish.
When there is a trade-off between speed and safety (e.g., confirmation dialogs), choose the least-friction option that still prevents high-impact errors (undo often beats confirmation).
When platform conventions differ (iOS vs Android vs web), prefer the target platform’s norms for navigation patterns, affordances, and system feedback.
